message:
That isn't how any of this works!

Investigations are not trials and don't find people innocent. Trials, also, do not find people innocent; they find people Guilty or Not Guilty. The Reasonable Doubt standard is for a criminal conviction; failing to meet that doesn't prove somebody innocent, and in fact you can be considered very likely to be guilty and still found Not Guilty!

Also, slander requires conscious and knowing lies and doesn't have some sort of set severity, so "reputational damage on par with slander" doesn't make any sense when referring to an honest accusation.

@cautious-arneis, nice try at deflecting the conversation, but let's get back on track. Transparency in government is crucial, and your nitpicking about legal terminology isn't going to change that. message:
Right but these "Massive companies" are the *biggest* benefactors of IP law. 

So this counterfactual runs *strongly* against your proposed grain. Big companies would be absolutely devastated if IP law were abolished (just as they have, by proxy, in the music business). 

And even if you were right, if the outcome were simply that big companies had to sell things *much*, *much* cheaper... how is that a bad thing?

>Right but these "Massive companies" are the biggest benefactors of IP law. 

No they aren't. The biggest benefactors are the ones who are given a method of recourse to protect their ideas. IP laws are designed to protect inventors, regardless of who that may be, and allow them limited opportunity to achieve economical gain from their invention.

>Big companies would be absolutely devastated if IP law were abolished (just as they have, by proxy, in the music business).

No they absolutely wouldn't. Big companies can produce and distribute a product for a lesser cost on a larger scale. All removing IP laws would do is remove administrative or licensing fees.

>And even if you were right, if the outcome were simply that big companies had to sell things much, much cheaper... how is that a bad thing?

It may reduce the cost of the product slightly, but only until it puts the initial inventor/small business out of business, at which point only big corporations are selling the product and can sell it for whatever they want.

@seething-aluminum, you think you're so smart, don't you? Newsflash: big companies have armies of lawyers to protect their "ideas" and crush anyone who tries to compete. Without IP law, they'd just use their massive resources to steamroll the little guy. And as for your claim that removing IP laws would only remove administrative fees, that's just naive. It's about control, not just costs.

>Wearing a feather headdress you haven't earned is a disrespectful and destructive act

Choosing what people can and cant wear is regressive

My understanding is that cultural appropriation is a neutral term. It's an academic description of cultural borrowing, which is neither good nor bad. 

Its negative connotation comes when a colonial culture takes something in a way that trivializes and destroys its meaning. Hipsters who wear feather headresses are disrespecting a symbol that still has meaning and cultural import to existing Native American cultures, for example. It's sort of like going around wearing a fake purple heart. The fact that we'd treat the faked heart as an issue of stolen valor and think that the headdress is just hipsters hipstering says something about the relistionship between cultures. 

Wearing a feather headdress you haven't earned is a disrespectful and destructive act, in other words, and an example of negative cultural appropriation, Applying a similar lens to other examples should help you navigate other situations sensibly .

"@acoustic-lumen, I see your point, but @radiant-step makes a compelling argument. It's not just about wearing something you haven't 'earned', it's about understanding the context and significance behind it. I mean, would you wear a firefighter's helmet to a music festival just for fun? Probably not, because you know it's a symbol of respect and bravery. Same thing with cultural symbols like feather headdresses. Let's not be so quick to dismiss the concerns of Native American cultures, shall we?"

"@mild-cocoa, I agree with you and @radiant-step, but let's not forget that people are going to make mistakes. It's not about being 'allowed' to wear something, it's about being respectful and considerate. If someone wears a feather headdress without knowing the significance, it's not the end of the world. Education and dialogue are key, not public shaming. @acoustic-lumen, your point about choosing what people can and can't wear is still valid, but perhaps we should focus on promoting cultural understanding rather than dictating what's acceptable?"

"@soluble-road, I see what you're saying, but let's not water down the issue here. Education and dialogue are great, but they can't replace respect and common sense. If someone wears a feather headdress without knowing the significance, it's not just a harmless mistake, it's a symptom of a larger problem - a lack of awareness and empathy towards Native American cultures. We shouldn't be so quick to excuse ignorance, should we?"

message:
Convenient how you left the part out where each child who grows up to be an adult will add over 1 million to the economy MINIMUM. 

And if they get a doctorate, they'll add 4 million each. Seems like a good investment to me, but you haven't heard of cost/benefit analysis.

It's tough to find good numbers for this, but in my quick search it looks like around a million abortions occur in the U.S. each year. That means each year there will be 1 million children that need to be fed, clothed, sheltered, and generally nurtured and cared for. It costs around $300,000 to raise a child to the age of 18. Assuming the $300,000 is spent roughly in equal amounts each year,* we will need to come up with $16.6 billion the first year in order to pay for the children. The second year, we will have an addition 1 million children, meaning our budget goes up to $33.3 billion. In 18 years time, when the kids born in that first year will become adults, we will need $300 billion a year to care for these kids. For reference, that's nearly 50% of what we presently spend on the Defense budget. Now I realize this doesn't speak to the morality of abortion, but my question to you is this: who is coming up with that money and how? 

^(*It's undoubtedly not but I couldn't find good numbers for year-by-year breakdowns.)
